metadata.title: Why Rewriting the Wrong Fix Makes the Build Worse, Not Better
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Teachers candidate 09. The mechanism: forward correction after a failed step accumulates context pollution — Claude reasons against the failure history as if it is constraint; /rewind restores conversation and file system to the checkpoint before the failure, and a respecified prompt with the failure mode as an explicit negative constraint executes cleanly.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = /rewind / clean state / respecification; CRIMSON = forward correction / context pollution / compounding failure; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO git revert comparison, NO /clear versus /rewind distinction in depth, NO context-window token math, NO formal discussion of context pollution beyond the intuition.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The teacher's handoff condition fired. The new About page has a broken link. Their instinct: ask Claude to fix it. They ask once. The fix introduces a second issue. They ask again. The build is now worse than when it failed.

card.copy: Why rewriting the wrong fix makes the build worse, not better

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] rewriting wrong fix makes build worse

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] rewriting wrong fix makes build worse

beats.narration_text: Here is the session. Handoff condition: every link resolves on the school server. The About page's link to the syllabus 404s. The link uses a page-relative path that worked on local dev. Teacher asks Claude: fix the broken link. Claude changes it to dot-slash-syllabus-dot-html. Still wrong. Still relative. The handoff fires again.

production_viz.label: first correction attempt: wrong, adds to context
production_viz.mechanic: a vertical session log growing: 'Step: add About page [PASS]'; 'Handoff: link 404s [FAIL]'; 'Fix request: fix the broken link'; 'Claude output: ./syllabus.html'; 'Handoff: link 404s again [FAIL]'; each FAIL line in crimson; session bar at right showing growing context

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] session log growing: two failures, context accumulating

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. Iteratively asking Claude to fix its own mistakes should converge on a correct solution. Here is the session where two forward-correction attempts compounded into a worse state than the original failure. Why?

card.copy: Two fixes. Worse than the original failure. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] the question on screen

beats.narration_text: Forward correction after a failed step pollutes the context. The session now contains the original failure, the first failed fix, the second failure. Claude's next prompt operates against all of it as if it is part of the desired result. The failure history becomes constraint. The context is pushing Claude toward variations on what failed — not toward the clean solution that was never tried.

production_viz.label: context pollution: failure history treated as constraint
production_viz.mechanic: a context bar filling with crimson blocks labeled 'original failure', 'failed fix 1', 'failed fix 2'; a label 'Claude reasons against this'; a teal block at bottom labeled 'original spec' shrinking; a note 'the clean solution was never tried'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] context bar: failures piling on, original spec shrinking

beats.narration_text: Slash-rewind. Claude Code restores both the conversation and the file system to the checkpoint before the failed step. The failures are gone — from both Claude's memory and your files. The session context returns to the state where the build was working. Then you respecify: the original specification plus the failure mode as an explicit negative constraint.

production_viz.label: /rewind: conversation + file system restored to checkpoint
production_viz.mechanic: a split diagram: left 'before /rewind' — crimson context with failures; right 'after /rewind' — teal clean context matching the checkpoint; an arrow from left to right labeled '/rewind'; a note at bottom 'conversation + file system both restored'

beats.narration_text: The respecification. Original specification did not mention link path format. The respecification adds: all link paths must use absolute server-relative paths with a leading slash; never use page-relative paths. That one constraint — which the failure revealed — is the difference between the second attempt working and repeating the first.
beats.scene_description: a printed specification document with a handwritten correction adding a negative constraint about link paths, editorial collage, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B06, a printed specification document on aged paper with a handwritten line added below the existing text, the handwritten addition clearly an amendment or correction, pinned to newsprint, editorial collage style, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones, no human faces, no readable text
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] specification document with handwritten negative constraint added

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] specification document with handwritten negative constraint added

beats.narration_text: The two-failure rule. After two failed corrections on the same step, clear the session entirely. Two failures is the signal that the context is polluted beyond what slash-rewind alone can clean. Start fresh. Rewrite the specification with both failures named as explicit negative constraints. The third attempt, with both failures in the spec, usually works.

production_viz.label: two-failure rule: after two corrections, /clear and rewrite from scratch
production_viz.mechanic: a decision tree: 'handoff fails' -> 'first correction' -> 'handoff fails again' -> 'second correction' -> 'two failures: /clear + rewrite spec'; branch at first failure: 'success' goes teal; failure path stays crimson; the /clear node is teal

beats.narration_text: The practical heuristic. The cost of slash-rewind feels high in the moment. It is consistently lower than the cost of continuing with forward correction. The compounding from polluted context is what most often turns a 15-minute bug into a 90-minute one. The respecification is not starting over. It is starting over with the failure as a known constraint.

production_viz.label: forward correction: 90-minute compounding; /rewind + respec: 15 minutes resolved
production_viz.mechanic: two horizontal time bars: left 'forward correction' — grows from '15 min' to '90 min' in crimson with annotations 'failure 1', 'failure 2', 'failure 3'; right '/rewind + respec' — stays at 15 min in teal; labels 'polluted context compounds' vs 'clean state, one constraint added'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] time cost: forward correction 90 min vs /rewind 15 min

beats.narration_text: The handoff catches the failure. Slash-rewind removes the failure from context. The respecification carries the failure forward as a constraint. That is the whole discipline.

card.copy: Handoff catches it. /rewind removes it. Respec carries it forward.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — handoff catches, /rewind removes, respec carries forward

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — handoff catches, /rewind removes, respec carries forward
